summary: Gruber reviews iTunes 11's major visual redesign, finding it genuinely impressive while noting it's a cosmetic overhaul rather than a ground-up rewrite. The new design eliminates the cluttered sidebar in favor of a horizontal navigation bar, putting less in the user's face at once in an iOS-inspired approach. He highlights the Expanded View feature as particularly brilliant, with its algorithmically-chosen color palettes that make browsing feel like opening physical albums. While praising the main window redesign, he notes that dialog boxes, preferences, and other secondary interfaces remain largely unchanged from previous versions, creating an uneven experience.
key_points: iTunes 11 is a genuine visual redesign but not a rewrite — the underlying architecture remains the same while the presentation layer is completely new
The move from sidebar navigation to a horizontal bar with popovers is a significant improvement that better organizes disparate content types that were previously treated as peers
The Expanded View feature uses algorithmic color matching from album art to create a sense of opening physical media in place — a clever direct manipulation design
The MiniPlayer is now a separate window rather than a mode of the main window, which enables new flexibility but prevents animation between states
Secondary UI elements like dialog boxes, Get Info, and the modal Preferences window remain untouched relics from previous versions
money_quotes: There are redesigns and there are redesigns. This one's the real deal.
iTunes 11 is in many ways a redefinition of what it means to be a modern Mac app.
There's an iOS-inspired emphasis on putting less stuff in your face at the same time.
With the old sidebar interface, everything was treated, semantically, as a peer.
It's a digital approximation of going through real-world albums or DVD jewel boxes and opening them in place.
Does Apple have a single other remaining app with a modal preferences dialog? I have a sort of soft spot for it, though. It's like the last remaining vestige of classic Mac OS.
More credence to my hunch that Lucida Grande's days as the OS X system font are numbered.
key_insight: iTunes 11's redesign succeeds by applying iOS-era design principles — simplification, direct manipulation, and reduced visual clutter — to a notoriously bloated desktop app, even though it only transforms the surface while leaving the underlying complexity intact.
